Programmed instruction is mich in the educational news these days. What
is the prospect of using it in vocational and general agricultural education
at the secondary level, for collegiate education in agriculture and for
extension education in agriculture?
. This question is of interest to me as a field for exploration, having
just retired from active edministrative duties in agricultural education.
For about three years, I have been assembling a library of books and pan-
phlets in this field, and have been following ‘some of the pioneer projects
A_methods:
in programmed instruction under way et the Pennsylvania State University.
Dr. D. R. McCloy, Head of the Department of Agricultural Education in the
College of Agriculture, has been guiding some graduate student studies in
this field, Interesting results have been obtained.
From my resdings about programmed instruction, I have learned that the
Armed Forces and various industrial concerns are using this method of
instruction with what appears to be outstanding results. Of course there
are now many examples of the technique es it is applied in numerous academic
@isciplines in elementary, secondary, and collegiate education. At present,
however, there seems to have been very Little special application to the
broad field of agricultural education.

This leads me to ask the following questions:
1. Is there a need’ to explain or describe the basic principles of
programed instruction to educators in the field of agriculture?
2. As ea corallary of the preceding question, wuld agricultural

3. Would programmed instructional materials be a useful eddition

I would appreciate correspondence and your suggestions on any
phase of programmed instruction with special reference to

AATEA MEMBERSHIP AND DUES
Among materials distributed late this past spring by the 1962-63
President of AATBA, Clarence Bundy, was included a dues notice. It vas
hoped that those members who had not paid their 1963 dues would do s0.
However, a number of persons vho had already paid 1963 dues, completed
the dues information sheet end sent it with a check for $3.00 to the
treasurer. These have been applied to 1964 membership, and membership cards
will be distributed at the beginning of 1964, as in the past.
Mhe dues year may not be clear to all members. Since AATRA was
organized, the fiscal year (and dues year) bas been December 1 - November 30.
Thies made it possible to close the books and prepare a financial report for
the AATRA meeting during the AVA Convention in early December. Since the
terms of officers of AATEA now run from July 1 - June 30, to coincide with
those of AVA officers, consideration vas given last Decenber in Milvaukee to
changing the dues year to July 1 - June 30, also. After considerable
deliberation, it was decided by the executive committee and ratified by the
membership of AATBA that the dues year should remain December 1 - November 30.
In keeping with past practice, AATRA dues will be collected at the
AATRA breakfast during the AVA Convention in December, and those not present
will be contacted by mail shortly thereafter.
Vv. R. Cardozier, Treasurer
